macro_segments.title: Conclusion of Discussion on Bigdei Kehunah: Ma'aseh Rokeim versus Ma'aseh Choshev

micro_segments.title: Wrapping up textile terminology: Ma'aseh Rokeim (embroidery) and Ma'aseh Choshev (craftsman weaving)
Rabbi Eliezer's explanation: embroidery occurs where pre-designated; Rashi's account of the painting-then-embroidering process
Tanna in name of Rabbi Nechemia: Rokeim produces one image (needlework), Choshev produces two images (woven on both sides)

macro_segments.title: Inquiry into Who Wears the Eight Garments and Questions the Urim V'Tummim

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Only when the High Priest wears the eight garments can one inquire of the Urim V'Tummim; Biblical source from Bamidbar regarding Yehoshua and Elazar
Rav Dimi's ruling: The eight garments worn by the High Priest are also worn by the Mashuach Milchama (priest anointed for war)
Source from Vayikra: 'The holy garments of Aharon will be for his descendants after him'—interpreted to include anyone succeeding him in a position of authority

macro_segments.title: Challenges to the Mashuach Milchama Wearing Eight Garments: The Inheritance Question

micro_segments.title: Rav Adda bar Ahava's challenge: Can the son of the Mashuach Milchama inherit like the son of the High Priest?
Braita addressing who brings the Asirit Ha'eifah (daily meal-offering): Only one with inherited status—the High Priest, not the Mashuach Milchama
Verse on seven-day initiation (Vayikra): 'The priest who enters the Ohel Moed'—identifying whose status is inherited and who enters the Holy of Holies on Yom Kippur
Inference that Mashuach Milchama cannot enter the Holy of Holies; if he wears the eight garments, he should be able to—suggesting he does not wear them for temple service
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak's response: The verse about inherited status still refers only to the High Priest despite any theoretical capability of the Mashuach Milchama

macro_segments.title: Braita Denying the Mashuach Milchama's Clothing and Abaye's Reinterpretation

micro_segments.title: Braita: Mashuach Milchama neither wears four garments like a regular priest nor eight like the High Priest
Abaye's objection: This makes him like a non-priest; he must wear something to establish his priestly status
Abaye's resolution: He should wear eight garments like the High Priest, but the High Priest prevents it due to enmity (eivah) concerns
Rav Adda bar Ahava challenges Abaye: I will show you a Tanna who does not concern himself with eivah yet still denies the Mashuach Milchama the eight garments

macro_segments.title: Extended Braita on Distinctions between High Priest, Regular Priest, and Mashuach Milchama

micro_segments.title: Braita from Horiyos: Differences between High Priest and regular priest—special sin-offering (par), daily meal-offering (Asirit Ha'eifah), mourning restrictions, marriage restrictions
Braita continuation: Purity status, first choice of offerings, authority to jump ahead in service, wearing eight garments, exemption from temple impurity, Yom Kippur service
All privileges apply to a High Priest not anointed with oil; they continue to apply to a previous (retired) High Priest except for Yom Kippur offering and daily meal-offering
Only five things from Parashat Emor apply to the Mashuach Milchama: mourning restrictions, marriage restrictions, and (according to some) not returning the murderer from the city of refuge
The previous High Priest still wears eight garments and serves in the temple; the Mashuach Milchama does not wear garments—conclusive proof he does not wear them in the temple

macro_segments.title: Alternative Account: Mashuach Milchama Wears Eight Garments Only for Inquiring the Urim V'Tummim, Not for Temple Service

micro_segments.title: Ravin's version: The Mashuach Milchama wears eight garments not to perform temple service but to be asked about war decisions using the Urim V'Tummim
Braita supporting Ravin: 'The garments the High Priest wears, the Mashuach Milchama is asked in them'—for the purpose of inquiry, not for Avoda in the Beit HaMikdash

macro_segments.title: The Process of Inquiring the Urim V'Tummim: Voice, Question Format, and Divine Communication

micro_segments.title: Braita: How one asks the Urim V'Tummim—the inquirer faces the priest who faces the Shechina; responses are given in God's name
Question must be asked in a soft voice (like Chanah's prayer), not audibly loud, but verbalized not merely thought
Only one question at a time; if two are asked, only the first is answered; if time is pressing, two questions may be answered
Example: David's questions about Saul and Keilah; he asked out of order and received answers in the correct logical order
Decrees from Urim V'Tummim never fail, unlike prophetic decrees which may be reversed through repentance

macro_segments.title: The Meaning of Urim and Tummim; Dispute over Divine Communication Mechanism

micro_segments.title: Etymology: Urim (they give light), Tummim (they complete/make final); the letters on the stones would communicate God's word
Dispute: Rabbi Yochanan says letters protruded visibly; Reish Lakish says they recombined; problem of missing letters in tribal names
Resolutions: Rav Acha and Shmuel bar Yitzchak propose additional inscriptions (tribal names or patriarchs) to complete the alphabet
Braita: Only a priest with Divine Spirit and Shechina resting upon him can be asked; example of Evyatar—when he was asked and no answer came
Gemara: Ruach HaKodesh helps the Urim V'Tummim function; they are not automatic but require the priest's spiritual state
Gra's insight: Eli couldn't discern Chanah's silent prayer because letters that appeared (kaf-shin-resh-hey) could be misread as 'shikora' (drunk) instead of 'keshara' (proper)

macro_segments.title: Who May Be Asked via the Urim V'Tummim and the Categories of Inquirers

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Only a king (melech), Sanhedrin (or Av Beis Din), and those serving national needs may ask the Urim V'Tummim
Biblical source from Parashat Pinchas: Yehoshua ('he and all Israel with him') and Elazar the priest; identified as king, Mashuach Milchama, and Sanhedrin respectively

macro_segments.title: Structural Overview of Masechet Yoma: Seven Chapters of Avodah, Eighth Chapter of Laws for All Israel

micro_segments.title: Organization: Seven days before Yom Kippur, night before, then the Avoda of the day; now turning to laws outside the Beit HaMikdash that apply to everyone
Parallel to Masechet Shabbat: Both have seven chapters of foundations before reaching the core topic; eight represents the covenant (Brit Milah)

macro_segments.title: Prohibitions on Yom Kippur: Eating, Drinking, Bathing, Anointing, Wearing Shoes, and Sexual Relations

micro_segments.title: Mishna: Five categories of Yom Kippur prohibitions—eating, drinking, bathing, anointing, wearing shoes, and sexual relations; all subject to kareis (cutting off)
Exceptions: King and new bride may wash their faces (king for appearance before people, bride for appearance before husband)
Dispute: Rabbi Eliezer permits a woman in childbirth to wear shoes; Sages prohibit (or Sages allow, depending on reading); possible alternate meaning involving a midwife
Minimum amounts for violation: Eating the size of a pressed date with its pit; drinking the fill of one's cheeks (not the standard shiur of kezayit and revi'it)
Combination rules: Different foods combine toward the pressed-date measure; different drinks combine toward the cheek-fill measure; but eating and drinking do not combine with each other

macro_segments.title: The Word Assur (Forbidden) and the Debate over Chatzi Shiur (Half-Measure)

micro_segments.title: Gemara: Why does the Mishna open with 'assur' (forbidden) rather than describing only full violations? To include eating less than the minimum amount (chatzi shiur)
Dispute: Rabbi Yochanan holds chatzi shiur is biblically forbidden; Reish Lakish holds it is only rabbinically forbidden
Both agree it is forbidden (either biblically or rabbinically), but the Mishna's opening word 'assur' highlights this sub-minimum prohibition

macro_segments.title: Application of Chatzi Shiur Debate to Oath Violations and the Status of Rabbinically-Prohibited Acts

micro_segments.title: Mishnah context: Taking an oath 'I will not eat' and then eating non-kosher food—is the oath transgressed given one is already forsworn from Har Sinai regarding non-kosher food?
Solution (Rav, Shmuel, Rabbi Yochanan): When one says 'I will not eat' (kolel), the oath applies to the entire category including kosher foods, thus taking effect on non-kosher foods as well
Reish Lakish's position: An oath transgresses only if one explicitly swears not to eat a half-measure of forbidden food or uses language implying even tiny amounts
Implication: According to Reish Lakish, chatzi shiur (rabbinically forbidden) is not considered as biblically pre-sworn (mushba ve'omed) and an oath can take effect upon it

topical_tags.term: Ohel Moed (Tent of Meeting / Holy of Holies)
